content.full_text:
September 21, 1976

Mr. Matthew L. Hudson
City Attorney
City of Petaluma
P.O. Box 61
Post and English Streets
Petaluma, CA 94952

Dear Mr. Hudson:

Thank you for your letter of August 27, 1976 requesting advice concerning a possible conflict of interest involving a City Councilman sitting on the Community Development Commission and participating in matters relating to a redevelopment plan.

We generally agree with your interpretation of the Commission's holding in Opinion requested by William Owens, 2 FPPC Ops. 77 (No. 75-005, June 2, 1976), although we do not think it is a "street" from the position taken in Opinion requested by Thomas Quilesty, City Manager, City of Antioch, FPPC Ops. 71 (No. 75-083, July 2, 1975). Rather, it should be viewed as an interpretation of a different concept, that of what constitutes the "public generally" game that what will "foreseeably have a material financial effect." In any event, based on the facts presented in your letter, there appears to be no evidence that the matters involved in the adoption of a redevelopment plan will affect this Councilman's interest (which is a very limited one) in any "singular fashion."

Additionally, we believe there are reasons, independent of the reasoning in the Owen opinion, which lead us to conclude that Councilman Pruner's failure to disqualify himself in the instant matter would not constitute a violation of Government Code section 87100, et seq.

Government Code section 87103 defines "financial interest" (as used in the general prohibition of Government Code section 87100):

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

1/24

76-09-209

Dr. Matthew L. Hudson
September 21, 1970
Page Two


     (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000);

     (b) Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

     (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commer-
cial lending institution in the regular course of
business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to
the time when the decision is made; or

     (d) Any business entity in which the public official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management. For purposes of
this section, indirect investment or interest means
any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child of a public official, by an agent
on behalf of a public official, by any business
entity controlled by the public official or by a
trust in which he has a substantial interest. A
business entity is controlled by a public official
if the public official, his agents, spouse and
dependent children hold more than fifty percent of
the ownership interest in the entity. A public
official has a substantial interest in a trust when
the official, his spouse and dependent children
have a present or future interest worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000).

     Based on the information you have given us, it does
not appear "reasonably foreseeable" that adoption of the plan
will have a "material financial effect" on Mr. Bruner's in-
terests. This conclusion is premised on our understanding
that: a high percentage of Mr. Bruner's business is conducted
by mail and by telephone; he does not rely on "walk-in" cus-
tomers in the same way that a normal retail business does;
and he has no ownership interest, nor long-term lease arrange-
ment, in the area. I have attached an informal advice letter
previously issued by the Commission's staff under circumstances

Mr. Matthew L. Hudson
September 21, 1976
Page three


quite analogous to those you present. (See advice letter to
Mr. William Coats, issued May 18, 1976, attached.)

     Thank you for writing to the Commission. If we can
be of further assistance in this matter, please do not hesi-
tate to call me at (916) 322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                        Research Specialist

KWG:plh
Enclosure

CITY OF PETALUMA, California
POST AND ENGLISH STREETS 94952 · TELEPHONE (707) 763-2613

Office of the City Attorney
P. O. Box 61
August 27, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 897
Sacramento, CA 95804

Gentlemen: Attention: Mr. Ken Goshorn

Pursuant to the rules and regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission, I am asking for a letter opinion regarding the following facts:

The City of Petaluma has organized a Community Development Commission with the City Council sitting as the Commission. A project area has been adopted pursuant to the Health and Safety Code. A redevelopment plan for the project area has been prepared and is ready for action by the Commission and the City Council. Three of the Councilmen/Commissioners have possible conflicts of interest due to ownership of property or businesses owned by them doing business in the project area. Two of the Councilmen/Commissioners appear to fit directly the facts set forth in your Opinion #75-083 concerning the City of Antioch. They have consistently abstained from voting on the adoption of the plan. The third Councilman/Commissioner, however, seems more nearly to fit the facts in your Opinion #76-005 concerning the City of Davis. The third Councilman/Commissioner owns no property in the project area, however, he does have an oral month-to-month lease for office space within the project area from which he conducts his business as an insurance broker. His principle insurance business concerns farm-related insurance and personal fire and liability policies. He does almost no business-related insurance work.

Question: Would the third Councilman/Commissioner have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Political Reform Act of 1974

Fair Political Practices
Commission

-2-

August 27, 1976

such that he would be prohibited from taking part in the deci-
sion whether to adopt the proposed redevelopment plan?

I will be happy to supply you with any additional information
you may need. My analysis of the question and what I believe
to be the correct answer are contained in the attached memorandum
from me to Councilman Robert Brunner.

If you have any questions, please advise.

Very truly yours,

Matthew L. Hudson
City Attorney

MLH:mi
Encl.

such that he would be prohibited from taking part in the decision whether to adopt the proposed redevelopment plan?

I will be happy to supply you with any additional information you may need. My analysis of the question and what I believe to be the correct answer are contained in the attached memorandum from me to Councilman Robert Brunner.

We generally agree with your interpretation of the Commission's holding in Opinion requested by William Owen, 2 PPPC Ops. 77 (No. 76-005, June 2, 1976), although we do not think it is a "retreat" from the position taken in Opinion requested by Thomas Oglesby, City Manager, City of Antioch, 1 PPPC Ops. 71 (No. 75-083, July 2, 1975). Rather, it should be viewed as an interpretation of a different concept, that of what constitutes the "public generally" rather than what will "foreseeably have a material financial effect." In any event, based on the facts presented in your letter, there appears to be no evidence that the matters involved in the adoption of a redevelopment plan will affect this Councilman's interest (which is a very limited one) in any "singular fashion." Additionally, we believe there are reasons, independent of the reasoning in the Owen opinion, which lead us to conclude that Councilman Bruner's failure to disqualify himself in the instant matter would not constitute a violation of Government Code section 87100, et seq. Government Code Section 87103 defines "financial interest" (as used in the general prohibition of Government Code Section 87100): An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or (d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
